title: The Coordination Layer reference architecture
summary: Appendix B.1.1's canonical reference architecture. Two systems (A, B) do not communicate directly — a Coordination Layer sits between them, consults a Context Manager for Θ, produces an emergent output E, and a Feedback Loop closes back onto the context so coordination improves over time. This is the diagram every code template in the appendix is an instance of.
source: Appendix B.1.1 — The Coordination Layer Pattern (Three-Body Interface)

nodes.label: System A
nodes.description: First element — e.g. human expertise, edge model, generator

nodes.label: System B
nodes.description: Second element — e.g. AI model, cloud model, critic

nodes.label: Context Manager
nodes.description: Holds coordination context Θ — confidence, mode, history

nodes.label: Coordination Layer
nodes.description: Implements C(A, B, Θ). Interface adapters, coordination logic, emergence selection

nodes.label: Emergent Output
nodes.description: E — the coordinated result that exceeds max(A, B)

nodes.label: Feedback Loop
nodes.description: Records outcome quality, updates context, triggers learning

edges.description: Θ shapes how A and B are composed

identifiability_note: Reference architecture. Identifiability depends on the specific binding of A, B, Θ in a concrete deployment; the structural template itself is a specified design, not a measured claim.
